steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The question asks what the reference to the parlor as Tía Ana's "special province" accomplishes. The passage establishes the parlor as her territory — her school — now used nightly as Salomé's salon, with Tía Ana returning each morning to check whether guests have left so the room can revert to her classroom.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — It conveys that Tía Ana feels honored that her classroom is used for important events.: No mention of honor or reputation in the passage. ✗
  • Choice B — It characterizes the room as a place where Tía Ana can go to escape social pressures.: The text says nothing about Tía Ana seeking escape from social obligations. ✗
  • Choice C — It reveals that Tía Ana feels as if Salomé has betrayed her trust by allowing guests into a space she considers her own.: The tone doesn't support betrayal — her eagerness is territorial, not wounded. ✗
  • Choice D — It introduces an idea that helps explain Tía Ana's apparent eagerness for Salomé's guests to leave the space.: Calling the parlor her "special province" establishes her sense of ownership, which is precisely what makes her eager for guests to leave so the room can return to being hers. ✓
That is the correct answer.
